content: A rectangular coil of 250 turns has an average area of $20 \mathrm{~cm} \times 15 \mathrm{~cm}$. The coil rotates with a speed of 60 cycles per second in a uniform magnetic field of $2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~T}$ about an axis perpendicular to the field. The peak value of the induced emf is:
explanation:
Let’s collect the data and apply the standard formula for a rotating coil:

Given

• Number of turns: $N=250$

• Coil dimensions: $20\text{ cm}\times15\text{ cm}\implies A=0.20\times0.15=0.03\ \mathrm m^2$

• Rotation rate: $f=60$ Hz, so $\omega=2\pi f=120\pi\ \mathrm{rad/s}$

• Magnetic field: $B=2\times10^{-2}\ \mathrm T$

The induced emf varies as

$ \mathcal{E}(t)=N\,B\,A\,\omega\;\sin(\omega t) $

so the peak (maximum) emf is

$ \boxed{\mathcal{E}_{\max}=N\,B\,A\,\omega.} $

Substituting in:

$ \mathcal{E}_{\max} =250\times(2\times10^{-2})\times0.03\times(120\pi) =18\pi\ \mathrm V. $

Answer: $18\pi$ V, i.e. option C.

content: A circular coil of area $2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and resistance $2 \boldsymbol{\Omega}$ is arranged vertically in the east -west direction. A uniform magnetic field 0.2 T is set up across the plane in the north to east direction. Now the magnetic field is removed at a steady rate in 0.4 s What is the current developed in the coil?
explanation:
Let’s go step by step:

Convert the area into SI units:

$$A = 2\sqrt2\;\text{cm}^2 = 2\sqrt2\times10^{-4}\;\mathrm m^2.$$

Orientation → only the component of B perpendicular to the coil’s plane contributes to the flux.

The coil’s plane is vertical, its normal is along north–south.

The field is in the horizontal N–E direction (i.e. 45° from north), so

$$B_\perp = B\cos45^\circ = 0.2\times\frac1{\sqrt2}.$$

Initial magnetic flux

$$\Phi = B_\perp\,A = 0.2\cdot\frac1{\sqrt2}\cdot\bigl(2\sqrt2\times10^{-4}\bigr) = 4\times10^{-5}\;\mathrm Wb.$$

It goes to zero in $\Delta t=0.4$ s, so the induced emf is

$$\mathcal E = \frac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t} = \frac{4\times10^{-5}}{0.4} = 1\times10^{-4}\;\mathrm V.$$

The coil’s resistance is $R=2\;\Omega$, hence the induced current

$$I = \frac{\mathcal E}{R} = \frac{1\times10^{-4}}{2} = 0.5\times10^{-4}\;\mathrm A = 5\times10^{-5}\;\mathrm A.$$

Answer (Option B):  $0.5\times10^{-4}$ A.

content: A current of 2 A is passed through the primary coil. The total flux linked with the secondary coil, which is closely wound over the primary is $2000 \times 10^{-6}$ weber. What is the induced emf in the secondary if the current through the primary increases at a rate of $0.2 \mathrm{As}^{-1}$ ?
explanation:
First, recall that the induced emf in the secondary is

$$\mathcal{E} \;=\; -\frac{d\Lambda}{dt}$$

where $\Lambda$ is the total flux linkage. If the flux linkage is proportional to the primary current $I$, then

$$\Lambda = k\,I\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{d\Lambda}{dt}=k\,\frac{dI}{dt}$$

and since $k=\Lambda/I$, we get

$$\bigl|\mathcal{E}\bigr| \;=\;\frac{\Lambda}{I}\,\frac{dI}{dt}\,. $$

Given:

• $\Lambda=2000\times10^{-6}$ Wb

• $I=2$ A

• $\dfrac{dI}{dt}=0.2$ A/s

Hence

$$\bigl|\mathcal{E}\bigr| =\frac{2000\times10^{-6}}{2}\times0.2 =1000\times10^{-6}\times0.2 =200\times10^{-6}\;{\rm V} =2\times10^{-4}\;{\rm V}\,. $$

Answer: Option A) $2\times10^{-4}$ V.

content: The magnetic flux $\phi$ through a stationary loop of wire having a resistance R varies with time as $\phi=4 t^2+3 t$. The average emf and total charge flowing in the loop in the time interval $t=0$ to $t=\tau$ respectively are
explanation:
First, recall that the induced emf is given by Faraday’s law

$$\mathcal{E}=-\frac{d\phi}{dt}\,. $$

Here

$$\phi(t)=4t^2+3t\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{d\phi}{dt}=8t+3\,, $$

so in magnitude

$$|\mathcal{E}|=8t+3\,. $$

Average emf (magnitude) over $0$ to $\tau$:

$ \langle\mathcal{E}\rangle =\frac{1}{\tau}\int_{0}^{\tau}(8t+3)\,dt =\frac{1}{\tau}\Bigl[4t^2+3t\Bigr]_{0}^{\tau} =4\tau+3\,. $

Total charge $Q$ that flows in that time (using $I=\mathcal{E}/R$, $dQ=I\,dt$):

$ Q=\frac{1}{R}\int_{0}^{\tau}(8t+3)\,dt =\frac{1}{R}\bigl[4\tau^2+3\tau\bigr]\,. $

Hence the correct choice is Option D:

Average emf = $4\tau+3$,

Total charge = $\dfrac{4\tau^2+3\tau}{R}$.

content: A conducting circular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field $$\mathrm{B}=0.125 \mathrm{~T}$$ with its plane perpendicular to the loop. If the radius of the loop is made to shrink at a constant rate of $$2 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$$, then the induced emf when the radius is $$4 \mathrm{~cm}$$ is
explanation:
First, we need to find the rate at which the area of the loop is changing since the induced emf in the loop is related to the change of magnetic flux through the loop.

The magnetic flux $$\Phi$$ through the loop is given by:

$$\Phi = B \cdot A$$

where

$$B$$ is the magnetic field

and

$$A$$ is the area of the loop.

The area $$A$$ of a circular loop with radius $$r$$ is given by:

$$A = \pi r^2$$

The rate of change of the area $$A$$ with respect to time $$t$$ is:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \pi \frac{d}{dt} (r^2) = \pi \cdot 2r \cdot \frac{dr}{dt}$$

We are given:

  • $$\frac{dr}{dt} = -2 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} = -2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$$

  • $$r = 4 \mathrm{~cm} = 0.04 \mathrm{~m}$$

Substituting these values in, we get:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \pi \cdot 2r \cdot \frac{dr}{dt}$$

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \pi \cdot 2 \cdot 0.04 \cdot -2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \pi \cdot 2 \cdot 0.04 \cdot -2 \cdot 10^{-3}$$

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = -1.6 \times 10^{-4} \pi \mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$$

The induced emf $$\varepsilon$$ is given by Faraday's law of induction:

$$\varepsilon = - \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

Since$$ \Phi = B \cdot A$$, we have:

$$\varepsilon = - B \cdot \frac{dA}{dt}$$

Substituting the values of $$B$$ and $$\frac{dA}{dt}$$, we get:

$$\varepsilon = -0.125 \mathrm{~T} \cdot -1.6 \times 10^{-4} \pi \mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$$

$$\varepsilon = 0.125 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \pi$$

$$\varepsilon = 2 \times 10^{-5} \pi \mathrm{~V}$$

Since the question asks for the answer in $$\mu V$$ (microvolts), we convert the emf:

$$\varepsilon = 20 \pi \mu \mathrm{V}$$

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option B: $$20 \pi \mu V$$

content: A transformer of $$100 \%$$ efficiency has 200 turns in the primary and 40000 turns in the secondary. It is connected to a $$220 \mathrm{~V}$$ main supply and secondary feeds to a $$100 \mathrm{~K} \Omega$$ resistance. The potential difference per turn is
explanation:
To find the potential difference per turn in the transformer, we need to understand the primary and secondary voltage relationship. Given that the transformer has a high efficiency, we will assume it's ideal for simplicity.

The transformer formula relating the number of turns and the voltage in primary and secondary windings is given by:

$$\frac{V_p}{V_s} = \frac{N_p}{N_s}$$

Where:

  • $$V_p$$ is the primary voltage
  • $$V_s$$ is the secondary voltage
  • $$N_p$$ is the number of turns in the primary winding
  • $$N_s$$ is the number of turns in the secondary winding

Given:

  • $$V_p = 220 \, \mathrm{V}$$
  • $$N_p = 200$$
  • $$N_s = 40000$$

We need to find $$V_s$$, the secondary voltage. Rearrange the formula to solve for $$V_s$$:

$$V_s = V_p \times \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

Substitute the given values:

$$V_s = 220 \, \mathrm{V} \times \frac{40000}{200}$$

Calculate $$V_s$$:

$$V_s = 220 \, \mathrm{V} \times 200 = 44000 \, \mathrm{V}$$

Now, we need to find the potential difference per turn. For an ideal transformer, this is the same for both primary and secondary because the voltage per turn is consistent in an ideal transformer. We can use the primary coil to find this:

Potential difference per turn (in primary) is:

$$\frac{V_p}{N_p}$$

Substitute the values for $$V_p$$ and $$N_p$$:

$$\frac{220 \, \mathrm{V}}{200 \, \text{turns}} = 1.1 \, \mathrm{V \, per \, turn}$$

Therefore, the potential difference per turn is:

Option D: 1.1 V

content: The current in a coil changes steadily from $$3 \mathrm{~A}$$ to $$5 \mathrm{~A}$$ in $$0.2 \mathrm{~s}$$ when an emf of $$2 \mu \mathrm{V}$$ is induced in it. The self-inductance of the coil is
explanation:
The self-inductance of the coil can be determined using the formula for the induced emf in a coil due to a changing current. The formula is given by:

$$ \mathcal{E} = -L \frac{dI}{dt} $$

Where:

  • $$\mathcal{E}$$ is the induced emf
  • $$L$$ is the self-inductance of the coil
  • $$\frac{dI}{dt}$$ is the rate of change of current

We are given the following values:

$$ \mathcal{E} = 2 \mu \mathrm{V} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{V} $$

Initial current, $$ I_1 = 3 \mathrm{~A} $$

Final current, $$ I_2 = 5 \mathrm{~A} $$

Time interval, $$ \Delta t = 0.2 \mathrm{~s} $$

The rate of change of current can be calculated as:

$$ \frac{dI}{dt} = \frac{I_2 - I_1}{\Delta t} = \frac{5 \mathrm{~A} - 3 \mathrm{~A}}{0.2 \mathrm{~s}} = \frac{2 \mathrm{~A}}{0.2 \mathrm{~s}} = 10 \mathrm{~A/s} $$

Now, using the formula for induced emf:

$$ 2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{V} = L \times 10 \mathrm{~A/s} $$

Solving for $$L$$:

$$ L = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{V}}{10 \mathrm{~A/s}} $$

$$ L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~H} $$

$$ L = 0.2 \mu \mathrm{H} $$

Therefore, the self-inductance of the coil is 0.2 $$\mu \mathrm{H}$$. So, the correct option is:

Option D: 0.2 $$\mu H$$

content: The magnetic flux linked with a coil is given by the equation: $$\phi=8 t^2+t+10$$. The e.m.f. induced in the coil in the $$3^{\text {rd }}$$ second will be

A wire ' 1 ' $$\mathrm{cm}$$ long bent into a circular loop is placed perpendicular to the magnetic field of flux density '$$B^{\prime} W b \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$$. Within $$0.1 \mathrm{sec}$$, the loop is changed into a square of side '$$a$$' $$\mathrm{cm}$$ and flux density is doubled. The value of e.m.f. induced is
explanation:
We start by noting that the induced emf (by Faraday’s law) is given by

$$ \varepsilon = -\frac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t}, $$

where the flux change

$$ \Delta\Phi = \Phi_{\text{final}} - \Phi_{\text{initial}}. $$

In our problem the magnetic field is perpendicular to the loop (so we don’t have to worry about any angle factors) and its value doubles when the loop is changed in 0.1 sec. Let’s set up the steps:

1. Define l as the length of the wire (in cm) and a as the side of the square (in cm) into which the wire is reformed. (In many problems the wire length l is used; here the given value “1 cm” is simply a particular case, so we write our answer in terms of l and a.)

2. When the wire is bent into a circle, its circumference is l. That means the radius r of the circle is

$$ r = \frac{l}{2\pi}. $$

Therefore, the area of the circular loop is

$$ A_{\text{circle}} = \pi r^2 = \pi\left(\frac{l}{2\pi}\right)^2 = \frac{l^2}{4\pi}. $$

3. When the loop is changed into a square of side a, its area is simply

$$ A_{\text{square}} = a^2. $$

(Note: In a typical wire‐reshaping problem the new shape’s perimeter would equal l so that a would be l/4. Here, however, the side a is given explicitly, so we use it as an independent parameter.)

4. The initial magnetic flux is that through the circular loop in a field B:

$$ \Phi_{\text{initial}} = B\,A_{\text{circle}} = B\,\frac{l^2}{4\pi}. $$

After 0.1 sec the loop is made into a square and, at the same time, the magnetic flux density is doubled to 2B. Therefore the final flux is

$$ \Phi_{\text{final}} = (2B)\,A_{\text{square}} = 2B\,a^2. $$

5. Thus the change in flux is

$$ \Delta\Phi = \Phi_{\text{final}} - \Phi_{\text{initial}} = 2B\,a^2 - B\,\frac{l^2}{4\pi}. $$

6. Plug this into Faraday’s law with the time interval $ \Delta t = 0.1\,\text{sec} $:

$$ \varepsilon = -\frac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t} = -\frac{2B\,a^2 - \dfrac{B\,l^2}{4\pi}}{0.1}. $$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 10 (since $1/0.1 = 10$) gives

$$ \varepsilon = -10B\left(2a^2 - \frac{l^2}{4\pi}\right). $$

7. To combine the terms inside the parentheses, rewrite $2a^2$ with a common denominator:

$$ 2a^2 = \frac{8\pi\,a^2}{4\pi}. $$

Thus we have

$$ \varepsilon = -10B\,\left(\frac{8\pi\,a^2 - l^2}{4\pi}\right) = -\frac{10B}{4\pi}\,(8\pi\,a^2 - l^2). $$

Simplify the factor $\frac{10}{4} = \frac{5}{2}$, so

$$ \varepsilon = -\frac{5B}{2\pi}\,(8\pi\,a^2 - l^2). $$

This is exactly the expression given in option C:

$$ \textbf{Option C:}\quad \varepsilon = \frac{-5B\,(8\pi a^2 - l^2)}{2\pi}. $$

Thus, the induced emf is

$$ \varepsilon = -\frac{5B\,(8\pi\,a^2 - l^2)}{2\pi}, $$

and the correct answer is Option C.

content: In an inductor of self-inductance $$2 \mathrm{~mH}$$, current changes with time (in sec) according to the relation, $$I=(3 t^2-3 t+8) A$$. The emf becomes zero at
explanation:
To determine when the emf is zero, follow these steps:

Recall that the induced emf in an inductor is given by:

$$\varepsilon = -L \frac{dI}{dt}.$$

Since the self-inductance $$L$$ is a nonzero constant, the emf will be zero when the derivative $$\frac{dI}{dt}$$ is zero.

Differentiate the given current equation with respect to time:

$$I(t) = 3t^2 - 3t + 8.$$

Calculating the derivative:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = 6t - 3.$$

Set the derivative equal to zero and solve for $$t$$:

$$6t - 3 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 6t = 3 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5 \, \text{sec}.$$

Thus, the emf becomes zero at 0.5 sec, which corresponds to Option C.

content: A metallic rod of length '$$a$$' is rotated with an angular frequency of $$0.2 \mathrm{~rads}^{-1}$$ about an axis normal to the rod passing through its one end. A constant and uniform magnetic field of '$$\mathrm{B}$$' T parallel to the axis exists everywhere. The emf developed across the ends of the rod is
explanation:
The emf (electromotive force) induced across the ends of a rotating rod in a magnetic field can be calculated using Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, considering the motion of the rod through the magnetic field.

When the rod rotates about an axis perpendicular to its length with angular frequency $$\omega$$, each element of the rod at a distance $$r$$ from the axis experiences a tangential velocity $$v = r\omega$$. According to the magnetic flux cutting principle, the emf $$d\mathcal{E}$$ induced in a small element of the rod of length $$dr$$ is given by:

$$ d\mathcal{E} = B \cdot v \cdot dr = B \cdot (r\omega) \cdot dr $$

where $$B$$ is the magnetic field.

Integrate this expression from 0 to $$a$$ (the length of the rod) to find the total emf $$\mathcal{E}$$ across the rod:

$$ \mathcal{E} = \int_0^a B \cdot r\omega \cdot dr $$

Perform the integration:

$$ \mathcal{E} = B \omega \int_0^a r \, dr = B \omega \left[ \frac{r^2}{2} \right]_0^a = B \omega \cdot \frac{a^2}{2} $$

Given that $$\omega = 0.2 \ \text{rads}^{-1}$$, substitute this value into the equation:

$$ \mathcal{E} = B \cdot 0.2 \cdot \frac{a^2}{2} = \frac{B a^2 \cdot 0.2}{2} $$

Simplify the expression:

$$ \mathcal{E} = \frac{B a^2}{10} $$

Therefore, the emf developed across the ends of the rod is:

Option A:

$$ \frac{\mathrm{Ba}^2}{10} $$

content: Select the unit of the coefficient of mutual induction from the following.
explanation:
The coefficient of mutual induction, commonly denoted by $ M $, is defined by the relationship between the induced electromotive force (EMF) in one coil and the rate of change of current in another coil. According to Faraday's Law of Induction, the induced EMF ( $ \mathcal{E} $ ) in a coil is given by:

$$ \mathcal{E} = -M \frac{dI}{dt} $$

Where:

  • $ \mathcal{E} $ is the induced EMF in volts (V).
  • $ M $ is the mutual inductance.
  • $ \frac{dI}{dt} $ is the rate of change of current in amperes per second (A/s).

Rearranging this equation to solve for $ M $, we get:

$$ M = - \frac{\mathcal{E}}{\frac{dI}{dt}} $$

From this relationship, the units of $ M $ can be derived. The unit of EMF ( $ \mathcal{E} $ ) is volts (V), and the unit of $ \frac{dI}{dt} $ is amperes per second (A/s). Thus, the unit of the coefficient of mutual induction is:

$$ M = \frac{\text{V}}{\text{A/s}} = \text{volt} \cdot \text{second} / \text{ampere} $$

Therefore, the correct unit for the coefficient of mutual induction is:

Option A: volt. second / ampere

content: Around the central part of an air cored solenoid of length $$20 \mathrm{~cm}$$ and area of cross section $$1.4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2$$ and 3000 turns, another coil of 250 turns is closely wound. A current $$2 \mathrm{~A}$$ in the solenoid is reversed in $$0.2 \mathrm{~s}$$, then the induced emf produced is
explanation:
The magnetic field inside the solenoid is given by

$$B = \mu_0 nI = \mu_0 \frac{N}{L} I$$

where, $$N$$ is the number of turns, $$L$$ is the length of the solenoid, $$I$$ is the current flowing through the solenoid, and $$\mu_0$$ is the permeability of free space.

The magnetic flux through the smaller coil is given by

$$\phi = BA = \mu_0 \frac{N}{L} I A$$

where $$A$$ is the area of the smaller coil.

The induced emf in the smaller coil is given by Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction as

$$e = -\frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

Substituting the value of $$\phi$$ in the above equation, we get

$$e = -\frac{d}{dt} (\mu_0 \frac{N}{L} I A) = -\mu_0 \frac{N}{L} A \frac{dI}{dt}$$

Given that the current is reversed in $$0.2 s$$, the rate of change of current is

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \frac{2-(-2)}{0.2} = 20 \mathrm{~A/s}$$

Substituting the values, we get

$$e = -4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{3000}{0.2} \times 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 = \boxed{1.32 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~V}}$$

Therefore, the induced emf produced is $$1.32 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~V}$$.

content: A metallic rod of $$2 \mathrm{~m}$$ length is rotated with a frequency $$100 \mathrm{~Hz}$$ about an axis passing through the centre of the circular ring of radius $$2 \mathrm{~m}$$. A constant magnetic field $$2 \mathrm{~T}$$ is applied parallel to the axis and perpendicular to the length of the rod. The emf developed across the ends of the rod is :
explanation:
To determine the electromotive force (emf) developed across the ends of the rotating rod, we can use the concept of motional emf generated in a rotating conductor in a magnetic field.

The formula for the motional emf in a rotating rod is given by:

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} B \omega L^2 $$

where:

  • $$B$$ is the magnetic field strength
  • $$\omega$$ is the angular velocity
  • $$L$$ is the length of the rotating rod

First, let's find the angular velocity $$\omega$$. The frequency $$f$$ is given as $$100 \, \text{Hz}$$. The relationship between angular velocity $$\omega$$ and frequency $$f$$ is:

$$ \omega = 2 \pi f $$

Plugging in the values:

$$ \omega = 2 \pi \cdot 100 = 200 \pi \, \text{rad/s} $$

The length of the rod $$L$$ is given as $$2 \, \text{m}$$ and the magnetic field strength $$B$$ is given as $$2 \, \text{T}$$.

Now, substituting these values into the motional emf formula:

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot 200 \pi \cdot (2)^2 $$

Simplifying this expression:

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot 200 \pi \cdot 4 $$

$$ \varepsilon = 800 \pi \, \text{V} $$

Therefore, the emf developed across the ends of the rod is:

Option A: 800 $$ \pi $$ volt

content: The magnetic flux linked with a coil satisfies the relation $$\phi=\left(4 t^2+6 t+9\right) \mathrm{Wb}$$, where $$t$$ is time in second. The emf induced in the coil at $$t=2 \mathrm{~s}$$ is
explanation:
Given, $$\phi=\left(4 t^2+6 t+9\right) \mathrm{Wb}$$ and $$t=2 \mathrm{~s}$$

We know that, $$|\varepsilon|=\left|\frac{d \phi}{d t}\right|$$

Here, $$\left|\frac{d \phi}{d t}\right|=|8 t+6| \Rightarrow\left(\frac{d \phi}{d t}\right)_{t=2}=8 \times 2+6$$

Induced emf in the coil $$=22 \mathrm{~V}$$

content: One volt induced emf is produced in the secondary coil when the current through the primary coil is changed from $$3 \mathrm{~A}$$ to $$1 \mathrm{~A}$$ in 100 milliseconds. the mutual inductance of the two coil is:
explanation:
To find the mutual inductance (M) between the two coils given the induced emf (voltage change) in the secondary coil when the current in the primary coil changes, we can use the formula for induced emf due to mutual inductance:

$$ emf = -M \frac{\Delta I}{\Delta t} $$

where:

  • $ emf $ is the induced electromotive force (in volts, V)
  • $ M $ is the mutual inductance between the coils (in henries, H)
  • $ \Delta I $ is the change in current (in amperes, A)
  • $ \Delta t $ is the time over which the current changes (in seconds, s)

Given values are:

  • $ emf = 1 \, \mathrm{V} $
  • $ \Delta I = I_{final} - I_{initial} = 1 \, \mathrm{A} - 3 \, \mathrm{A} = -2 \, \mathrm{A} $
  • $ \Delta t = 100 \, \mathrm{milliseconds} = 100 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{s} = 0.1 \, \mathrm{s} $

Plugging these values into the formula, we solve for $ M $:

$$ 1 = -M \frac{-2}{0.1} $$

$$ M = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.1 $$

$$ M = 0.05 \, \mathrm{H} $$

Therefore, the mutual inductance of the two coils is 0.05 H, which corresponds to Option D.

content: Television frequencies are of the order of 100 MHz, while radio frequencies are of the order of 1 MHz. Using these as typical frequencies, the ratio of the emf generated in a loop antenna by a television wave to that generated by a radio wave, if both have equal electric field intensities.
explanation:
As we know that, emf induced $$e=\frac{d \phi_B}{d t}$$

or $$e=\frac{dB}{dt}$$ (when area is constant $$\phi_B \propto B$$)

Now for television wave, $$e_1=\frac{d}{d t}(B)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{d}{d t}\left(B_0 \sin \left(2 \pi f_1 t+\phi\right)\right) \\ e_1 & =B_0 2 \pi f_1 \cos \left(2 \pi f_1 t+\phi\right) \end{aligned}$$

For radiowave, $$e_2=\frac{d}{d t}(B)$$

$$\begin{aligned}& =\frac{d}{d t}\left(B_0 \sin \left(2 \pi f_2 t+\phi\right)\right) \\ = & B_0 2 \pi f_2 \cos \left(2 \pi f_2+\phi\right) \end{aligned}$$

The ratio of emf induced

$$=\frac{e_1}{e_2}=\frac{B_0 2 \pi f_1 \cos \left(2 \pi f_1 t+\phi\right)}{B_0 2 \pi f_2 \cos \left(2 \pi f_2 t+\phi\right)}$$

Since, $$f_1=100 \mathrm{MHz}, f_2=1 \mathrm{MHz}$$ and $$\cos \left(2 \pi f_1 t+\phi\right)=\cos \left(2 \pi f_2 t+\phi\right)=\cos \phi$$ (For complete cycle)

We get, $$\frac{e_1}{e_2}=\frac{100}{1}=100$$

content: A circular coil of 20 turns and radius 10 cm is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.10 T normal to the plane of the coil. If the current in the coil is 5 A, then the average force on each electron in the coil due to the magnetic field is
explanation:
Magnetic Lorenz force acting on each electron,

$$F=evB$$ ..... (i)

As, current, $$i=neAv$$

$$ev=\frac{i}{nA}$$

Therefore, substituting value of $$ev$$ into Eq. (i),

$$F=\frac{i}{nA}B$$

Given, $$i=5~A,n=20$$ turns, $$B = 0.1$$ T

$$F = eVB = {{iB} \over {nA}} = {{5 \times 0.1} \over {{{10}^{29}} \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = 5 \times {10^{ - 25}}N$$

content: A coil of wire of a certain radius has 100 turns and a self inductance of 15 mH. The self inductance of a second similar coil of 500 turns will be
explanation:
Given, number of turns in the first coil, $$N_1=100$$

Its self-inductance, $$L_1=15 \mathrm{mH}=1.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{H}$$

Number of turns in the second coil,

$$\begin{array} & N_2 & =500 \\ L_2 & =\text { ? }\end{array}$$

We know that, self inductance of the coil is given by

$$L=\frac{\mu_0\pi N^2R}{2}$$

$$\Rightarrow L \propto N^2$$

$$\therefore \frac{L_2}{L_1} =\left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2=\left(\frac{500}{100}\right)^2=25$$

$$\Rightarrow L_2 =25 L_1$$

$$=25 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$=37.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{H}$$

$$=375 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}=375 ~\mathrm{mH}$$

content: A coil of 100 turns carries a current of 5 mA and creates a magnetic flux of 10$$^{-5}$$ Wb. The inductance is
explanation:
Given, number of turns, $$N=100$$

Current, $$I=5 \mathrm{~mA}=5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$$

Magnetic flux, $$\phi=10^{-5} \mathrm{~Wb}$$

Inductance, $$L=$$ ?

We know that,

$$\begin{aligned}N \phi & =L I \\ \Rightarrow \quad L & =\frac{N \phi}{I}=\frac{100 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & =20 \times 10^{-2} ~\mathrm{H}=0.2 ~\mathrm{H} \end{aligned}$$

content: In step-up transformer, relation between number of turns in primary (N$$_P$$) and number of turns in secondary (N$$_S$$) coils is
explanation:
In step-up transformer, number of turns in secondary coil is greater than the number of turns in the primary coil.

i.e. N$$_S$$ > N$$_P$$
